title: Impro is a handbook for running a cult
excerpt: Here’s the big idea in Keith Johnstone’s book Impro:
content_html:
Here’s the big idea in Keith Johnstone’s book Impro:

  1. Children are naturally creative, but are violently formed into repressed adults by Western culture and education
  2. The process of becoming more creative and expressive is largely a process of unlearning these habits of repression
  3. Improv — improvisational comedy — is thus not just the skeleton key for learning to act, but for unlocking a more authentically human way of life

This take doesn’t sound particularly original, but references to Impro pop up in all kinds of places: in influential tech blogs, as part of the initial process of onboarding for Palantir, and on the reading list of multiple big-tech founders. Impro is part of the secret canon of Silicon Valley, right alongside books like Seeing Like a State and The Power Broker. Why is that? For two reasons: first, because Johnstone’s outsider critique of established institutions is appealing; and second, because Impro is a handbook for running a cult.

Defense mechanisms and status

The part of Impro that is most obviously useful to software engineers is Johnstone’s chapter on status.

According to him, status games pervade all social interactions. Even innocuous, friendly conversations operate in terms of status. When you apologize or downplay something to “be nice”, that’s performing low status; when you reassure somebody, that’s performing high status; when you and a friend are comparing stories, you’re making friendly bids for status from each other. In the workplace, these status games are conditioned by the formal status of your role: you must allow your boss the high status position most of the time, or you’ll be (correctly) perceived as insubordinate. This is understood in some cultures, where it’s often called “face”, but in Western cultures it’s taboo to openly discuss status games.

The core social skill is the ability to deliberately alter your status. Someone who can only perform low status is a weak person, pitiable, annoying. Someone who can only perform high status is a braggart, a posturer, dangerous. To be effective socially, you must be able to switch between high and low status when appropriate, sometimes from sentence to sentence. I wrote about this exact point at the end of Big tech engineers need big egos: effective senior+ software engineers must be able to present as high status in order to be useful authorities, but also to switch to low status in order to take direction from the company leaders.

As an example, Johnstone describes in detail how he manipulates status in the classroom. He begins by sitting on the floor (deliberately assuming low status), and explaining that if his students fail, it’s his fault not theirs, since he’s the expert. The initial low status puts the class at ease, but in his words, ”[my] actual status is going up, since only a very confident and experienced person would put the blame for failure on himself.” These skills are not just useful for improv comedy.

Improvisation as a lifestyle choice

Impro is not just a book about improvising well. It’s a book about how you should live your life. In other words, Johnstone thinks that everyone would be better off if they became more spontaneous and ditched their shells of over-analysis. He criticizes the culture of Western thought in a number of different areas. According to him:

  • Everyone is more or less equivalently mentally ill, but “sane” people simply have better coping mechanisms
  • Cities and “taking pills” (read: antidepressants) are obscene, but you should be able to make sexual jokes in the workplace and generally be uninhibited
  • If we were free from the puritanical shackles of Western culture, childbirth would not be painful1

Johnstone didn’t come up with these ideas — they’re standard counterculture positions from the 1960s and 1970s — but it goes to show how he connected improvisational comedy to this general anti-establishment political program. Johnstone ran his classes and theatre troupe like a revolutionary cadre. Here are some quotes from Something Like a Drug: An Unauthorized Oral History of Theatresports:

So of course when I was invited to join Loose Moose Theatre and train at improvisational games late at night in an abandoned garage in a run-down portion of the city, I was thrilled. I remember thinking, This is a revolutionary act.

Keith [Johnstone] got a group of his more talented students together to start improvising outside of school hours. Usually in his basement.

The Secret Impro group—it’s very strange. It was very much that Keith said we were going to do this, and we’d just do it. It was like we were sheep. Keith would say when we were going to do a show, and we’d just do it, blindly. Like I said, if we had the videotapes now, we’d be very embarrassed and probably never go on stage again. We became a group of people who would follow Keith. There was always that sort of “tag” put on those people who were with Keith and those people who were against Keith. We were the people, basically, that if he said something, we believed it.

To some extent, it’s plausible that teaching acting or improvisation requires a high level of trust in your teacher. When Johnstone says things like “Students need a ‘guru’ who ‘gives permission’ to allow forbidden thoughts into their consciousness.”, I can believe that it’s just how you have to teach acting. But the more I read of Impro (and particularly when I read Something Like a Drug and Johnstone’s biography Keith Johnstone), the less it sounded like an ordinary book on acting.

Instead, it began to sound like a charismatic man who had found a way to gather a group of disciples that would let him mold their psyches. In other words, it began to sound like a cult.

Masks, cults and theatre groups

Impro was first introduced to the software world by Venkatesh Rao (of Gervais Principle fame), who wrote a brief review. Rao gives a detailed account of the first three-quarters of Impro, but glosses right over the last chapter, called “Masks and Trance”, simply saying “despite the disturbing raw material, the ideas and concepts are not particularly difficult to grasp and accept”. What ideas and concepts?

Johnstone’s discussion of masks (or “Masks”, in his language — he always capitalizes the word) is as explicitly cult-like as Impro gets. In brief, Johnstone has a box of literal, physical prop masks. He introduces the box with great ceremony to his students2, warning them seriously about the dangers of possession and reassuring them that he is a skilled and competent spirit guide. Through various hypnosis-adjacent techniques3 (Johnstone draws the parallel quite explicitly) he conditions his students to be in a trance state when wearing a mask, and believes this produces more authentic emotional states in their acting and improvisation.

Here are some quotes from the book:

A high-status person whom you accept as dominant can easily propel you into unusual states of being. You’re likely to respond to his suggestion…

Once you understand that you’re no longer held responsible for your actions, then there’s no need to maintain a ‘personality’.

One famous French teacher of the Mask—who won’t approve of this essay4—divides students immediately into those who can work Masks and those who can’t.

I don’t cast an actor to play a Masked role until I know he has the ability to become ‘possessed’.

It’s true that an actor can wear a Mask casually, and just pretend to be another person, but Gaskill and myself were absolutely clear that we were trying to induce trance states.

Johnstone has a long and painful explanation of how new mask-wearers seem to mentally regress to the point where they don’t know how to open umbrellas or interact with chairs. He describes one student always going to the bathroom before putting on a mask, because she’s worried she might wet herself. New mask-wearers are non-verbal must be taught to speak again.

If this were at the beginning of the book, I think it would turn a lot of people off. But by the time you get to it, I suspect most readers are already warmed up enough to say “sure, why not, it seems weird but I guess it works”. Not me!

Johnstone attempts to defuse the obvious weirdness by arguing that trance states are very common (e.g. being lost in a book). More unconvincingly, he says this in response to the worry that vulnerable people are going to get mentally harmed:

As for the fear of madness, I would answer that the ability to become possessed is a sign of correct social adjustment, and that really disturbed people censor themselves out. Either they can’t do it, or they’re afraid to even try. People who feel themselves at risk avoid situations where they feel likely to ‘go to pieces’.

Does this convince anyone? Mentally vulnerable people fall into dangerous situations all the time: ayahuasca trips, cults, GPT-4o, and so on. It’s such a weak argument.

In general, I’m struck by the sheer power Johnstone held over his disciples. He has them yell slurs at each other, encourages them to feel deep emotions in quick succession, relax any mental defenses and regress to a childhood state, and literally hypnotizes them. He explicitly lays out his procedure for breaking down their sense of self:

The stages I try to take students through involve the realisation (1) that we struggle against our imaginations, especially when we try to be imaginative; (2) that we are not responsible for the content of our imaginations; and (3) that we are not, as we are taught to think, our ‘personalities’, but that the imagination is our true self.

If your imagination is your true self, and you’re not responsible for its content, you’re not ultimately responsible for anything: you’re in the safe hands of the guru, who can mold you as he wishes. Later on, Johnstone walks it back a bit:

In the end they learn how to abandon control while at the same time they exercise control. … You have to misdirect people to absolve them of responsibility. Then, much later, they become strong enough to resume the responsibility themselves.

So the explicit idea is that (much later), the guru hands autonomy back to his disciples, when they’re ready to take it. This does not exactly reassure me, particularly against the background noise of everyone in Johnstone’s circle saying “boy I sure love being part of this cult!”

What kind of cult leader was Johnstone?

I don’t think Johnstone was preying on his students. The strongest evidence against this is that he did marry a student5, Ingrid Brind. That’s not great! On the other hand, it was fairly standard for professors back then — when I was in grad school for philosophy, several of my older male6 professors had wives that they’d taught decades ago — so I don’t think it proves Johnstone was that kind of cult leader.

I even read Ann Jellicoe’s play The Knack to get a better picture of Johnstone’s character. Jellicoe had an affair with Johnstone for several years, and his official biography claims7 that the character of Tom in The Knack is directly based on Johnstone. The Knack is a rather unpleasant play about sexual assault, but Tom’s character is largely asexual: he’s certainly no feminist, but is much more interested in impressing people with his intelligence than with getting laid.

In Something Like a Drug, two women who were part of Loose Moose, Johnstone’s Canadian improv group, describe their experiences:

You know, it brings around the other question: Why do the guys get laid after the show and not the chicks? You know, I can remember those days when Tony [Totino] and Dave [Duncan] and all those guys … the women would swarm around them. Those were the days, my friend.

In Loose Moose I think there are fewer women not only because of the training, but because of the guys in Loose Moose. When I came up with Joanne and Laura, there was a real initiation that was going on, and there was a group of guys at that time who were all single. And they would hit on you to the point where one night Joanne, Laura and I, who really didn’t know each other, were in a show together, started talking and realized that we were getting the same pickup lines from the same guys. And that’s when you realize what’s going on, and I think that’s intimidating. Or if a woman gets into a relationship with a senior improvisor and it doesn’t work out or something bad happens. I think that’s one reason.

This dynamic doesn’t sound great, but it doesn’t mention Johnstone, and it doesn’t sound particularly unusual: I’ve heard versions of this story about all kinds of ordinary male-dominated nerd spaces.

In fact, reading through the anecdotes in Something Like a Drug is a good antidote to the cultish atmosphere in Impro. Johnstone’s argument goes something like: “if we could only throw away the restrictive chains of Western culture and permit ourselves to be as obscene and free as children, we would be transported to a better, more beautiful world”. Well, you tried that, and the women in the group are still relegated to playing bimbos and housewives, there are still petty personal fights, and the guru is out here union-busting8. What was enlightenment supposed to look like?

I think the most generous defense of Johnstone is that his group was not unusually cult-like, and that any similar account from one of his peer improv teachers would raise the same red flags. Maybe improv classes and groups (particularly in the 70s and 80s) were just cultish in general? Having now read four books on Johnstone, I’m reluctant to go and read more to prove or disprove this theory, but it’s at least plausible.

Cults and startups

To anyone familiar with San Francisco software engineering culture, it should be pretty clear why Impro is so popular. The line between a startup and a cult is very thin indeed.

In his book Zero to One, Peter Thiel famously says that good startups are “slightly less extreme kinds of cults”. If you believe that, it makes total sense to assign Impro as mandatory reading for new Palantir hires. It tells them what kind of cult you’re trying to run: one where you’ll disregard existing cultural norms, learn to play status games well, think on your feet, and generally be molded by the guru into a more persuasive, more effective engineer.

Read critically, Impro also serves as a handbook for engineers who are trying to recognize if the environment they’re in is cult-like. Is your company telling you to reinvent your personality in order to be better at your job? Are you under the spell of a charismatic, high-status leader? Is your company trying to keep you in an unquestioning flow trance state?

Conclusion

In the great battle between the shackles of restrictive culture and the glorious freedom of the guru, I am always and forever on the side of the shackles of restrictive culture. In general, I think most boring and stupid social norms (such as not hypnotizing and marrying your students) serve an important purpose and shouldn’t just be cut down in the name of freedom.

Impro is still a good book. There’s a lot to learn from Johnstone’s analysis of power dynamics, of education, and of creativity in general. By all accounts he was excellent at teaching students how to improvise. But I wouldn’t recommend adopting it as your life philosophy, and I’d recommend being a bit suspicious of anyone pushing this book too hard. Getting rid of the existing social structures might benefit confident, wildly charismatic gurus like Johnstone, but most of us are just ordinary animals who do better in a group governed by norms.

  1. In fairness to Johnstone, he cites Sheila Kitzinger’s The Experience of Childbirth in support of this claim (the others he just puts in his own words), so maybe he felt that this was a bit out there. As you would expect, the pain of childbirth is a universal biological fact.

    ↩

  2. Concerningly, the description in Something Like a Drug (in the foreword) suggests that this class was unofficial.

    ↩

  3. As an example, he prompts the masked student to relax, then startles him with a mirror to trigger the trance state.

    ↩

  4. Probably Jacques Lecoq.

    ↩

  5. See page 83 of Keith Johnstone: A Critical Biography.

    ↩

  6. I suppose that’s redundant.

    ↩

  7. On page 51 of Keith Johnstone: A Critical Biography (it’s called “critical” but it was clearly written with Johnstone’s involvement and support, and does not seriously criticize him at any point). In The Knack, Tom gives a monologue about how to teach children to play the piano that could be lifted straight out of Impro.

    ↩

  8. In 1983 Johnstone “read the riot act” to the improv players who were planning to unionize, threatening that they’d be cut out of the group for good. To quote Dennis Cahill, a group member at the time who opposed the union: “I just didn’t see the point to it. … I didn’t really see a need to confront Keith or cause Keith problems or to upset him in any way over something as simple as Who Has The Power or Who Doesn’t.”

The Dictator's Handbook and the politics of technical competence

The Dictator’s Handbook is an ambitious book. In the introduction, its authors Bruce Bueno de Mesquita and Alastair Smith cast themselves as the successors to Sun Tzu and Niccolo Machiavelli: offering unsentimental advice to would-be successful leaders.

Given that, I expected this book to be similar to The 48 Laws of Power, which did not impress me. Like many self-help books, The 48 Laws of Power is “empty calories”: a lot of fun to read, but not really useful or edifying. However, The Dictator’s Handbook is a legitimate work of political science, serving as a popular introduction to an actual academic theory of government.

In The Knack, Tom gives a monologue about how to teach children to play the piano that could be lifted straight out of Impro. ↩ In 1983 Johnstone “read the riot act” to the improv players who were planning to unionize, threatening that they’d be cut out of the group for good. To quote Dennis Cahill, a group member at the time who opposed the union: “I just didn’t see the point to it. … I didn’t really see a need to confront Keith or cause Keith problems or to upset him in any way over something as simple as Who Has The Power or Who Doesn’t.” ↩ If you liked this post, consider subscribing to email updates about my new posts, or sharing it on Hacker News.Here's a preview of a related post that shares tags with this one.The Dictator's Handbook and the politics of technical competenceThe Dictator’s Handbook is an ambitious book. In the introduction, its authors Bruce Bueno de Mesquita and Alastair Smith cast themselves as the successors to Sun Tzu and Niccolo Machiavelli: offering unsentimental advice to would-be successful leaders.Given that, I expected this book to be similar to The 48 Laws of Power, which did not impress me. Like many self-help books, The 48 Laws of Power is “empty calories”: a lot of fun to read, but not really useful or edifying. However, The Dictator’s Handbook is a legitimate work of political science, serving as a popular introduction to an actual academic theory of government.Continue reading...

blocks.html_en: This take doesn’t sound particularly original, but references to Impro pop up in all kinds of places: in influential tech blogs, as part of the initial process of onboarding for Palantir, and on the reading list of multiple big-tech founders. Impro is part of the secret canon of Silicon Valley, right alongside books like Seeing Like a State and The Power Broker. Why is that? For two reasons: first, because Johnstone’s outsider critique of established institutions is appealing; and second, because Impro is a handbook for running a cult.

blocks.html_en: The part of Impro that is most obviously useful to software engineers is Johnstone’s chapter on status.

blocks.html_en: According to him, status games pervade all social interactions. Even innocuous, friendly conversations operate in terms of status. When you apologize or downplay something to “be nice”, that’s performing low status; when you reassure somebody, that’s performing high status; when you and a friend are comparing stories, you’re making friendly bids for status from each other. In the workplace, these status games are conditioned by the formal status of your role: you must allow your boss the high status position most of the time, or you’ll be (correctly) perceived as insubordinate. This is understood in some cultures, where it’s often called “face”, but in Western cultures it’s taboo to openly discuss status games.

sentences.en: According to him, status games pervade all social interactions.
sentences.zh: 他认为，地位游戏渗透到所有社交互动中。

sentences.en: Even innocuous, friendly conversations operate in terms of status.
sentences.zh: 即使是无伤大雅、友好的对话也会影响地位。

sentences.en: When you apologize or downplay something to “be nice”, that’s performing low status; when you reassure somebody, that’s performing high status; when you and a friend are comparing stories, you’re making friendly bids for status from each other.
sentences.zh: 当你为了“友善”而道歉或淡化某些事情时，那就是表现低下；当你让某人放心时，那就是表现出很高的地位；当你和朋友比较故事时，你们是在友好地争取彼此的地位。

sentences.en: In the workplace, these status games are conditioned by the formal status of your role: you must allow your boss the high status position most of the time, or you’ll be (correctly) perceived as insubordinate.
sentences.zh: 在工作场所，这些地位游戏取决于你的角色的正式地位：你必须在大部分时间让你的老板处于高地位，否则你会（正确地）被视为不服从命令。

sentences.en: This is understood in some cultures, where it’s often called “face”, but in Western cultures it’s taboo to openly discuss status games.
sentences.zh: 这在某些文化中是可以理解的，通常被称为“面子”，但在西方文化中，公开讨论地位游戏是禁忌。

blocks.html_en: The core social skill is the ability to deliberately alter your status. Someone who can only perform low status is a weak person, pitiable, annoying. Someone who can only perform high status is a braggart, a posturer, dangerous. To be effective socially, you must be able to switch between high and low status when appropriate, sometimes from sentence to sentence. I wrote about this exact point at the end of Big tech engineers need big egos: effective senior+ software engineers must be able to present as high status in order to be useful authorities, but also to switch to low status in order to take direction from the company leaders.

sentences.en: To be effective socially, you must be able to switch between high and low status when appropriate, sometimes from sentence to sentence.
sentences.zh: 为了在社交上发挥作用，你必须能够在适当的时候在高地位和低地位之间切换，有时是从一句话到句子。

sentences.en: I wrote about this exact point at the end of Big tech engineers need big egos: effective senior+ software engineers must be able to present as high status in order to be useful authorities, but also to switch to low status in order to take direction from the company leaders.
sentences.zh: 我在《大型科技工程师需要自负》的最后写到了这一点：高效的高级+软件工程师必须能够表现出较高的地位，才能成为有用的权威，但也必须能够转变为较低的地位，以便接受公司领导的指导。

blocks.html_en: As an example, Johnstone describes in detail how he manipulates status in the classroom. He begins by sitting on the floor (deliberately assuming low status), and explaining that if his students fail, it’s his fault not theirs, since he’s the expert. The initial low status puts the class at ease, but in his words, ”[my] actual status is going up, since only a very confident and experienced person would put the blame for failure on himself.” These skills are not just useful for improv comedy.

blocks.html_en: To some extent, it’s plausible that teaching acting or improvisation requires a high level of trust in your teacher. blocks.html_en: Johnstone’s discussion of masks (or “Masks”, in his language — he always capitalizes the word) is as explicitly cult-like as Impro gets. In brief, Johnstone has a box of literal, physical prop masks. He introduces the box with great ceremony to his students2, warning them seriously about the dangers of possession and reassuring them that he is a skilled and competent spirit guide. Through various hypnosis-adjacent techniques3 (Johnstone draws the parallel quite explicitly) he conditions his students to be in a trance state when wearing a mask, and believes this produces more authentic emotional states in their acting and improvisation.

blocks.html_en: In general, I’m struck by the sheer power Johnstone held over his disciples. He has them yell slurs at each other, encourages them to feel deep emotions in quick succession, relax any mental defenses and regress to a childhood state, and literally hypnotizes them. He explicitly lays out his procedure for breaking down their sense of self:

sentences.en: In general, I’m struck by the sheer power Johnstone held over his disciples.
sentences.zh: 总的来说，约翰斯通对他的门徒所拥有的绝对权力令我震惊。

sentences.en: He has them yell slurs at each other, encourages them to feel deep emotions in quick succession, relax any mental defenses and regress to a childhood state, and literally hypnotizes them.
sentences.zh: 他让他们互相辱骂，鼓励他们快速连续地感受到深刻的情感，放松任何心理防御并回归到童年状态，并真正催眠他们。

sentences.en: He explicitly lays out his procedure for breaking down their sense of self:
sentences.zh: 他明确地列出了打破他们自我意识的程序：

blocks.html_en: The stages I try to take students through involve the realisation (1) that we struggle against our imaginations, especially when we try to be imaginative; (2) that we are not responsible for the content of our imaginations; and (3) that we are not, as we are taught to think, our ‘personalities’, but that the imagination is our true self.

sentences.en: The stages I try to take students through involve the realisation (1) that we struggle against our imaginations, especially when we try to be imaginative; (2) that we are not responsible for the content of our imaginations; and (3) that we are not, as we are taught to think, our ‘personalities’, but that the imagination is our true self.
sentences.zh: 我试图引导学生经历的阶段包括认识到（1）我们与自己的想象力作斗争，尤其是当我们试图发挥想象力时； (2) 我们对我们的想象内容不负有责任； （3）我们并不是像我们被教导的那样认为我们的“个性”，而是想象才是我们真实的自我。

blocks.html_en: If your imagination is your true self, and you’re not responsible for its content, you’re not ultimately responsible for anything: you’re in the safe hands of the guru, who can mold you as he wishes. Later on, Johnstone walks it back a bit:

sentences.en: If your imagination is your true self, and you’re not responsible for its content, you’re not ultimately responsible for anything: you’re in the safe hands of the guru, who can mold you as he wishes.
sentences.zh: 如果你的想象力就是你的真实自我，并且你不对它的内容负责，那么你最终也不会对任何事情负责：你在导师的安全手中，他可以按照他的意愿塑造你。

sentences.en: Later on, Johnstone walks it back a bit:
sentences.zh: 后来，约翰斯通又回顾了一下：

blocks.html_en: In the end they learn how to abandon control while at the same time they exercise control. … You have to misdirect people to absolve them of responsibility. Then, much later, they become strong enough to resume the responsibility themselves.

sentences.en: In the end they learn how to abandon control while at the same time they exercise control. …
sentences.zh: 最终，他们学会了如何放弃控制，同时又行使控制权。 ……

sentences.en: Then, much later, they become strong enough to resume the responsibility themselves.
sentences.zh: 然后，很久以后，他们变得足够强大，可以自己承担起责任。

blocks.html_en: So the explicit idea is that (much later), the guru hands autonomy back to his disciples, when they’re ready to take it. This does not exactly reassure me, particularly against the background noise of everyone in Johnstone’s circle saying “boy I sure love being part of this cult!”

sentences.zh: 因此，明确的想法是（很久以后），当弟子准备好接受自主权时，大师将自主权交还给他们。

sentences.en: This does not exactly reassure me, particularly against the background noise of everyone in Johnstone’s circle saying “boy I sure love being part of this cult!”
sentences.zh: 这并不能完全让我放心，特别是在约翰斯通圈子里每个人都在说“孩子，我真的很喜欢成为这个邪教的一部分！”的背景噪音下。

blocks.html_en: What kind of cult leader was Johnstone?

sentences.en: What kind of cult leader was Johnstone?
sentences.zh: 约翰斯通是一个什么样的邪教领袖？

blocks.html_en: I don’t think Johnstone was preying on his students. The strongest evidence against this is that he did marry a student5, Ingrid Brind. That’s not great! On the other hand, it was fairly standard for professors back then — when I was in grad school for philosophy, several of my older male6 professors had wives that they’d taught decades ago — so I don’t think it proves Johnstone was that kind of cult leader.

sentences.en: I don’t think Johnstone was preying on his students.
sentences.zh: 我不认为约翰斯通在欺骗他的学生。

sentences.en: The strongest evidence against this is that he did marry a student5, Ingrid Brind.
sentences.zh: 反对这一点的最有力证据是他确实娶了一位学生英格丽德·布林德 (Ingrid Brind)。

sentences.en: That’s not great!
sentences.zh: 那不太好！

sentences.en: On the other hand, it was fairly standard for professors back then — when I was in grad school for philosophy, several of my older male6 professors had wives that they’d taught decades ago — so I don’t think it proves Johnstone was that kind of cult leader.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，这对于当时的教授来说是相当标准的——当我在哲学研究生院时，我的几位年长的男教授都有几十年前教过的妻子——所以我不认为这证明约翰斯通是那种邪教领袖。

blocks.html_en: I even read Ann Jellicoe’s play The Knack to get a better picture of Johnstone’s character. Jellicoe had an affair with Johnstone for several years, and his official biography claims7 that the character of Tom in The Knack is directly based on Johnstone. The Knack is a rather unpleasant play about sexual assault, but Tom’s character is largely asexual: he’s certainly no feminist, but is much more interested in impressing people with his intelligence than with getting laid.

sentences.en: I even read Ann Jellicoe’s play The Knack to get a better picture of Johnstone’s character.
sentences.zh: 我什至读了安·杰利科的戏剧《诀窍》，以更好地了解约翰斯通的角色。

sentences.en: Jellicoe had an affair with Johnstone for several years, and his official biography claims7 that the character of Tom in The Knack is directly based on Johnstone.

sentences.en: The Knack is a rather unpleasant play about sexual assault, but Tom’s character is largely asexual: he’s certainly no feminist, but is much more interested in impressing people with his intelligence than with getting laid.
sentences.zh: 《诀窍》是一部关于性侵犯的相当令人不快的戏剧，但汤姆的角色很大程度上是无性的：他当然不是女权主义者，但更感兴趣的是用他的智慧给人们留下深刻的印象，而不是上床。

blocks.html_en: In Loose Moose I think there are fewer women not only because of the training, but because of the guys in Loose Moose. When I came up with Joanne and Laura, there was a real initiation that was going on, and there was a group of guys at that time who were all single. And they would hit on you to the point where one night Joanne, Laura and I, who really didn’t know each other, were in a show together, started talking and realized that we were getting the same pickup lines from the same guys. And that’s when you realize what’s going on, and I think that’s intimidating. Or if a woman gets into a relationship with a senior improvisor and it doesn’t work out or something bad happens. I think that’s one reason.

I think that’s one reason.

sentences.en: In Loose Moose I think there are fewer women not only because of the training, but because of the guys in Loose Moose.
sentences.zh: 我认为在Loose Moose 中，女性人数较少，不仅是因为训练的原因，还因为Loose Moose 中的男性。

sentences.en: When I came up with Joanne and Laura, there was a real initiation that was going on, and there was a group of guys at that time who were all single.
sentences.zh: 当我想到乔安妮和劳拉时，真正的开始正在发生，当时有一群人都是单身。

sentences.en: And they would hit on you to the point where one night Joanne, Laura and I, who really didn’t know each other, were in a show together, started talking and realized that we were getting the same pickup lines from the same guys.
sentences.zh: 他们会和你搭讪，有一天晚上，乔安妮、劳拉和我，彼此并不认识，一起参加一场演出，开始交谈，然后意识到我们从同一个人那里得到了同样的搭讪台词。

sentences.en: And that’s when you realize what’s going on, and I think that’s intimidating.
sentences.zh: 当你意识到发生了什么的时候，我认为这很令人恐惧。

sentences.en: Or if a woman gets into a relationship with a senior improvisor and it doesn’t work out or something bad happens.
sentences.zh: 或者，如果一个女人与一位高级即兴表演者建立了关系，但没有成功或发生了一些不好的事情。

sentences.en: I think that’s one reason.
sentences.zh: 我认为这是原因之一。

blocks.html_en: This dynamic doesn’t sound great, but it doesn’t mention Johnstone, and it doesn’t sound particularly unusual: I’ve heard versions of this story about all kinds of ordinary male-dominated nerd spaces.

sentences.en: This dynamic doesn’t sound great, but it doesn’t mention Johnstone, and it doesn’t sound particularly unusual: I’ve heard versions of this story about all kinds of ordinary male-dominated nerd spaces.
sentences.zh: 这种动态听起来不太好，但它没有提到约翰斯通，而且听起来也不是特别不寻常：我听过这个关于各种普通男性主导的书呆子空间的故事版本。

blocks.html_en: In fact, reading through the anecdotes in Something Like a Drug is a good antidote to the cultish atmosphere in Impro. Johnstone’s argument goes something like: “if we could only throw away the restrictive chains of Western culture and permit ourselves to be as obscene and free as children, we would be transported to a better, more beautiful world”. Well, you tried that, and the women in the group are still relegated to playing bimbos and housewives, there are still petty personal fights, and the guru is out here union-busting8. What was enlightenment supposed to look like?

sentences.en: In fact, reading through the anecdotes in Something Like a Drug is a good antidote to the cultish atmosphere in Impro.
sentences.zh: 事实上，阅读《像毒品一样的东西》中的轶事是缓解鹰普邪教氛围的良药。

sentences.en: Johnstone’s argument goes something like: “if we could only throw away the restrictive chains of Western culture and permit ourselves to be as obscene and free as children, we would be transported to a better, more beautiful world”.
sentences.zh: 约翰斯通的论点是这样的：“如果我们能够抛开西方文化的束缚，让自己像孩子一样淫荡和自由，我们就会被带到一个更美好、更美丽的世界”。

sentences.en: Well, you tried that, and the women in the group are still relegated to playing bimbos and housewives, there are still petty personal fights, and the guru is out here union-busting8.
sentences.zh: 好吧，你试过了，但团体中的女性仍然被降级为扮演荡妇和家庭主妇，仍然存在琐碎的个人斗争，而且大师在这里破坏工会8。

sentences.en: What was enlightenment supposed to look like?
sentences.zh: 启蒙应该是什么样子？

blocks.html_en: I think the most generous defense of Johnstone is that his group was not unusually cult-like, and that any similar account from one of his peer improv teachers would raise the same red flags. Maybe improv classes and groups (particularly in the 70s and 80s) were just cultish in general? Having now read four books on Johnstone, I’m reluctant to go and read more to prove or disprove this theory, but it’s at least plausible.

sentences.zh: 我认为对约翰斯通最慷慨的辩护是，他的团体并不是异常的邪教，而且他的一位即兴表演老师同行的任何类似叙述都会引发同样的危险信号。

sentences.en: Maybe improv classes and groups (particularly in the 70s and 80s) were just cultish in general?
sentences.zh: 也许即兴表演课程和团体（特别是在 70 年代和 80 年代）总体上只是一种崇拜？

sentences.en: Having now read four books on Johnstone, I’m reluctant to go and read more to prove or disprove this theory, but it’s at least plausible.
sentences.zh: 现在已经读了四本关于约翰斯通的书，我不愿意再去读更多的书来证明或反驳这个理论，但它至少是合理的。

blocks.html_en: Cults and startups

sentences.en: Cults and startups
sentences.zh: 邪教和初创公司

blocks.html_en: To anyone familiar with San Francisco software engineering culture, it should be pretty clear why Impro is so popular. The line between a startup and a cult is very thin indeed.

sentences.en: To anyone familiar with San Francisco software engineering culture, it should be pretty clear why Impro is so popular.
sentences.zh: 对于任何熟悉旧金山软件工程文化的人来说，应该很清楚鹰普为何如此受欢迎。

sentences.en: The line between a startup and a cult is very thin indeed.
sentences.zh: 初创公司和邪教之间的界限确实很细。

blocks.html_en: In his book Zero to One, Peter Thiel famously says that good startups are “slightly less extreme kinds of cults”. If you believe that, it makes total sense to assign Impro as mandatory reading for new Palantir hires. It tells them what kind of cult you’re trying to run: one where you’ll disregard existing cultural norms, learn to play status games well, think on your feet, and generally be molded by the guru into a more persuasive, more effective engineer.

sentences.en: In his book Zero to One, Peter Thiel famously says that good startups are “slightly less extreme kinds of cults”.
sentences.zh: 彼得·蒂尔（Peter Thiel）在他的著作《从零到一》中提出了一句名言：优秀的初创企业是“稍微不那么极端的邪教”。

sentences.en: If you believe that, it makes total sense to assign Impro as mandatory reading for new Palantir hires.
sentences.zh: 如果您相信这一点，那么将 Impro 指定为 Palantir 新员工的必读读物是完全有道理的。

sentences.en: It tells them what kind of cult you’re trying to run: one where you’ll disregard existing cultural norms, learn to play status games well, think on your feet, and generally be molded by the guru into a more persuasive, more effective engineer.
sentences.zh: 它告诉他们你想要经营什么样的邪教：你将无视现有的文化规范，学会玩好地位游戏，独立思考，并且通常会被大师塑造成一个更有说服力、更有效的工程师。

blocks.html_en: Read critically, Impro also serves as a handbook for engineers who are trying to recognize if the environment they’re in is cult-like. Is your company telling you to reinvent your personality in order to be better at your job? Are you under the spell of a charismatic, high-status leader? Is your company trying to keep you in an unquestioning flow trance state?

sentences.en: Read critically, Impro also serves as a handbook for engineers who are trying to recognize if the environment they’re in is cult-like.
sentences.zh: 批判性地阅读，鹰普还可以作为工程师的手册，帮助他们试图识别自己所处的环境是否类似于邪教。

sentences.en: Is your company telling you to reinvent your personality in order to be better at your job?
sentences.zh: 您的公司是否要求您重塑自己的个性，以便更好地完成工作？

sentences.en: Are you under the spell of a charismatic, high-status leader?
sentences.zh: 您是否被一位魅力非凡、地位崇高的领导者迷住了？

sentences.en: Is your company trying to keep you in an unquestioning flow trance state?
sentences.zh: 你的公司是否试图让你处于一种毫无疑问的心流恍惚状态？

blocks.html_en: Conclusion

sentences.en: Conclusion
sentences.zh: 结论

blocks.html_en: In the great battle between the shackles of restrictive culture and the glorious freedom of the guru, I am always and forever on the side of the shackles of restrictive culture. In general, I think most boring and stupid social norms (such as not hypnotizing and marrying your students) serve an important purpose and shouldn’t just be cut down in the name of freedom.

sentences.en: In the great battle between the shackles of restrictive culture and the glorious freedom of the guru, I am always and forever on the side of the shackles of restrictive culture.
sentences.zh: 在限制性文化的枷锁与上师光荣的自由之间的伟大战斗中，我永远站在限制性文化的枷锁一边。

sentences.en: In general, I think most boring and stupid social norms (such as not hypnotizing and marrying your students) serve an important purpose and shouldn’t just be cut down in the name of freedom.
sentences.zh: 总的来说，我认为大多数无聊和愚蠢的社会规范（例如不催眠和与你的学生结婚）都有重要的目的，不应该仅仅以自由的名义被废除。

blocks.html_en: Impro is still a good book. There’s a lot to learn from Johnstone’s analysis of power dynamics, of education, and of creativity in general. By all accounts he was excellent at teaching students how to improvise. But I wouldn’t recommend adopting it as your life philosophy, and I’d recommend being a bit suspicious of anyone pushing this book too hard. Getting rid of the existing social structures might benefit confident, wildly charismatic gurus like Johnstone, but most of us are just ordinary animals who do better in a group governed by norms.

sentences.en: Impro is still a good book.
sentences.zh: Impro仍然是一本好书。

sentences.en: There’s a lot to learn from Johnstone’s analysis of power dynamics, of education, and of creativity in general.
sentences.zh: 约翰斯通对权力动态、教育和创造力的分析有很多值得学习的地方。

sentences.en: By all accounts he was excellent at teaching students how to improvise.
sentences.zh: 众所周知，他非常擅长教学生如何即兴创作。

sentences.en: But I wouldn’t recommend adopting it as your life philosophy, and I’d recommend being a bit suspicious of anyone pushing this book too hard.
sentences.zh: 但我不建议将其作为你的人生哲学，并且我建议对任何过度推动这本书的人持怀疑态度。

sentences.en: Getting rid of the existing social structures might benefit confident, wildly charismatic gurus like Johnstone, but most of us are just ordinary animals who do better in a group governed by norms.
sentences.zh: 摆脱现有的社会结构可能会让像约翰斯通这样自信、极具魅力的大师受益，但我们大多数人只是普通动物，在受规范约束的群体中表现得更好。

blocks.html_en:
The Dictator's Handbook and the politics of technical competence

The Dictator’s Handbook is an ambitious book. In the introduction, its authors Bruce Bueno de Mesquita and Alastair Smith cast themselves as the successors to Sun Tzu and Niccolo Machiavelli: offering unsentimental advice to would-be successful leaders.

Given that, I expected this book to be similar to The 48 Laws of Power, which did not impress me. Like many self-help books, The 48 Laws of Power is “empty calories”: a lot of fun to read, but not really useful or edifying. However, The Dictator’s Handbook is a legitimate work of political science, serving as a popular introduction to an actual academic theory of government.

However, The Dictator’s Handbook is a legitimate work of political science, serving as a popular introduction to an actual academic theory of government.

content_html_zh:
这是基思·约翰斯通 (Keith Johnstone) 所著《鹰普》(Impro) 中的重要理念：

  1. 孩子们天生具有创造力，但却被西方文化和教育暴力地塑造成受压抑的成年人
  2. 变得更有创造力和表现力的过程很大程度上是一个摆脱这些压抑习惯的过程
  3. 因此，即兴喜剧不仅是学习表演的万能钥匙，也是开启更真实的人类生活方式的万能钥匙

这种说法听起来并不是特别原创，但对鹰普的引用出现在各种地方：在有影响力的科技博客中，作为 Palantir 初始入职流程的一部分，以及在多个大型科技创始人的阅读列表中。 鹰普是硅谷秘密经典的一部分，与《像国家一样看待》和《权力掮客》等书籍并列。 这是为什么？ 原因有两个：首先，约翰斯通对既定机构的局外人批评很有吸引力；其次，因为鹰普是一本管理邪教的手册。

防御机制和状态

对于软件工程师来说，鹰普最明显有用的部分是 Johnstone 关于状态的章节。

他认为，地位游戏渗透到所有社交互动中。 即使是无伤大雅、友好的对话也会影响地位。 当你为了“友善”而道歉或淡化某些事情时，那就是表现低下；当你让某人放心时，那就是表现出很高的地位；当你和朋友比较故事时，你们是在友好地争取彼此的地位。 在工作场所，这些地位游戏取决于你的角色的正式地位：你必须在大部分时间让你的老板处于高地位，否则你会（正确地）被视为不服从命令。 这在某些文化中是可以理解的，通常被称为“面子”，但在西方文化中，公开讨论地位游戏是禁忌。

核心社交技能是有意识地改变自己的地位的能力。 只能表现出低地位的人是一个软弱的人，可怜的，令人讨厌的。 一个只能表现出高地位的人是一个自吹自擂的人，一个装腔作势的人，一个危险的人。 为了在社交上发挥作用，你必须能够在适当的时候在高地位和低地位之间切换，有时是从一句话到句子。 我在《大型科技工程师需要自负》的最后写到了这一点：高效的高级+软件工程师必须能够表现出较高的地位，才能成为有用的权威，但也必须能够转变为较低的地位，以便接受公司领导的指导。

作为一个例子，约翰斯通详细描述了他如何在课堂上操纵地位。 他首先坐在地板上（故意表现出较低的地位），并解释说，如果他的学生失败了，那是他的错，而不是他们的错，因为他是专家。 最初的低地位让全班同学感到轻松，但用他的话说，“我的实际地位正在上升，因为只有非常自信和经验丰富的人才会把失败归咎于自己。” 这些技能不仅对即兴喜剧有用。

即兴创作作为一种生活方式的选择

Impro 不仅仅是一本关于即兴创作的书。 这是一本关于你应该如何生活的书。 换句话说，约翰斯通认为，如果每个人变得更加自发并抛弃过度分析的外壳，他们的生活会更好。 他在许多不同领域批评西方思想文化。 据他说：

  • 每个人或多或少都患有精神疾病，但“理智”的人只是有更好的应对机制
  • 城市和“吃药”（读作：抗抑郁药）是淫秽的，但你应该能够在工作场所开性玩笑，并且通常不拘一格
  • 如果我们摆脱了西方文化清教徒式的束缚，分娩就不会那么痛苦1

这些想法并非约翰斯通提出的——它们是 20 世纪 60 年代和 1970 年代的标准反主流文化立场——但这表明了他如何将即兴喜剧与这一普遍的反建制政治计划联系起来。 约翰斯通像革命干部一样管理他的班级和剧团。 以下是《像毒品一样的东西：未经授权的戏剧运动口述历史》中的一些引述：

因此，当我受邀加入 Loose Moose Theatre 并在深夜在城市破败地区的一个废弃车库里训练即兴游戏时，我当然感到非常兴奋。 我记得我当时想，这是一个革命性的行为。
基思（约翰斯通）召集了一群更有才华的学生，在课外时间开始即兴创作。 通常在他的地下室。
秘密即兴小组——很奇怪。 基思说我们要做这件事，我们就这么做。 就像我们是羊一样。 基思会说我们什么时候要做一场演出，然后我们就会盲目地去做。 就像我说的，如果我们现在有了录像带，我们会非常尴尬，可能再也不会上台了。 我们成为了一群追随基思的人。 那些支持基思的人和反对基思的人总是被贴上这样的“标签”。 基本上，我们是这样的人，如果他说了些什么，我们就相信。

在某种程度上，教授表演或即兴创作需要对老师高度信任，这似乎是合理的。 当约翰斯通说“学生需要一位‘导师’来‘允许’禁止的想法进入他们的意识。”时，我相信这就是你教表演的方式。 但我对鹰普的了解越多（尤其是当我读到《像毒品一样的东西》和约翰斯通的传记《基思·约翰斯通》时），它听起来就越不像一本普通的表演书籍。

相反，它开始听起来像是一个有魅力的人找到了一种方法来聚集一群门徒，让他塑造他们的心灵。 换句话说，它开始听起来像一个邪教。

面具、邪教和剧团

Impro 最初是由 Venkatesh Rao（因 Gervais Principle 闻名）引入软件世界的，他写了一篇简短的评论。 拉奥详细介绍了鹰普的前四分之三，但对最后一章“面具与恍惚”进行了注释，只是说“尽管原材料令人不安，但思想和概念并不是特别难以掌握和接受”。 有哪些想法和理念？

约翰斯通对面具（或者用他的语言来说是“面具”——他总是把这个词大写）的讨论与鹰普一样明显带有邪教色彩。 简而言之，约翰斯通有一盒真实的、物理的道具面具。 他以隆重的仪式向学生们介绍这个盒子，严肃地警告他们拥有这个盒子的危险，并向他们保证他是一位熟练且称职的精神导师。 通过各种与催眠相关的技术3（约翰斯通相当明确地进行了类比），他让学生在戴着面具时处于恍惚状态，并相信这会在他们的表演和即兴创作中产生更真实的情感状态。

以下是书中的一些引述：

一个你认为占主导地位的高地位的人很容易让你进入不寻常的状态。 你可能会回应他的建议……
一旦你明白你不再对自己的行为负责，那么就没有必要维持“个性”。
一位著名的法国面具老师——他不同意这篇文章4——立即将学生分为可以戴面具的人和不能戴面具的人。
在我知道演员有“着魔”能力之前，我不会选他来扮演蒙面角色。
确实，演员可以随意戴上面具，假装成另一个人，但加斯基尔和我自己都非常清楚，我们试图引发恍惚状态。

约翰斯通对新戴口罩的人似乎精神退化到不知道如何打开雨伞或与椅子互动的程度进行了漫长而痛苦的解释。 他描述了一名学生在戴上口罩之前总是先去洗手间，因为她担心自己会弄湿自己。 新戴口罩的人不会说话，必须重新学会说话。

如果这是本书的开头，我想很多人都会感到厌烦。 但当你读到它时，我怀疑大多数读者已经足够热衷于说“当然，为什么不呢，这看起来很奇怪，但我想它是有效的”。 不是我！

约翰斯通试图通过认为恍惚状态很常见（例如迷失在一本书中）来化解明显的怪异现象。 更令人难以置信的是，他这样说是为了回应对弱势群体会受到精神伤害的担忧：

至于对疯狂的恐惧，我会回答说，被附身的能力是正确社会适应的标志，这确实扰乱了人们的自我审查。 他们要么做不到，要么不敢尝试。 感觉自己处于危险之中的人会避免出现他们认为可能“崩溃”的情况。

这能说服任何人吗？ 精神上脆弱的人总是会陷入危险的境地：死藤水之旅、邪教、GPT-4o 等等。 这是一个非常无力的论据。

总的来说，约翰斯通对他的门徒所拥有的绝对权力令我震惊。 他让他们互相辱骂，鼓励他们快速连续地感受到深刻的情感，放松任何心理防御并回归到童年状态，并真正催眠他们。 他明确地列出了打破他们自我意识的程序：

我试图引导学生经历的阶段包括认识到（1）我们与自己的想象力作斗争，尤其是当我们试图发挥想象力时； (2) 我们对我们的想象内容不负有责任； （3）我们并不是像我们被教导的那样认为我们的“个性”，而是想象才是我们真实的自我。

如果你的想象力就是你的真实自我，并且你不对它的内容负责，那么你最终也不会对任何事情负责：你在导师的安全手中，他可以按照他的意愿塑造你。 后来，约翰斯通又回顾了一下：

最终，他们学会了如何放弃控制，同时又行使控制权。 …… 你必须误导人们才能免除他们的责任。 然后，很久以后，他们变得足够强大，可以自己承担起责任。

因此，明确的想法是（很久以后），当弟子准备好接受自主权时，大师将自主权交还给他们。 这并不能完全让我放心，特别是在约翰斯通圈子里每个人都在说“孩子，我真的很喜欢成为这个邪教的一部分！”的背景噪音下。

约翰斯通是一个什么样的邪教领袖？

我不认为约翰斯通在欺骗他的学生。 反对这一点的最有力证据是他确实娶了一位学生英格丽德·布林德 (Ingrid Brind)。 那不太好！ 另一方面，这对于当时的教授来说是相当标准的——当我在哲学研究生院时，我的几位年长的男教授都有几十年前教过的妻子——所以我不认为这证明约翰斯通是那种邪教领袖。

我什至读了安·杰利科的戏剧《诀窍》，以更好地了解约翰斯通的角色。 杰利科与约翰斯通有多年的恋情，他的官方传记声称《The Knack》中汤姆的角色直接以约翰斯通为原型。 《诀窍》是一部关于性侵犯的相当令人不快的戏剧，但汤姆的角色很大程度上是无性的：他当然不是女权主义者，但更感兴趣的是用他的智慧给人们留下深刻的印象，而不是上床。

在《像毒品一样的东西》一书中，约翰斯通加拿大即兴表演团体 Loose Moose 的两名女性描述了她们的经历：

你知道，这又带来了另一个问题：为什么演出结束后是男人上床，而不是女人？ 你知道，我还记得托尼（托蒂诺）和戴夫（邓肯）以及所有这些人的那些日子…… 女人们会蜂拥而至。 那些日子，我的朋友。
我认为在Loose Moose 中，女性人数较少，不仅是因为训练的原因，还因为Loose Moose 中的男性。 当我想到乔安妮和劳拉时，真正的开始正在发生，当时有一群人都是单身。 他们会和你搭讪，有一天晚上，乔安妮、劳拉和我，彼此并不认识，一起参加一场演出，开始交谈，然后意识到我们从同一个人那里得到了同样的搭讪台词。 当你意识到发生了什么的时候，我认为这很令人恐惧。 或者，如果一个女人与一位高级即兴表演者建立了关系，但没有成功或发生了一些不好的事情。 我认为这是原因之一。

这种动态听起来不太好，但它没有提到约翰斯通，而且听起来也不是特别不寻常：我听过这个关于各种普通男性主导的书呆子空间的故事版本。

事实上，阅读《像毒品一样的东西》中的轶事是缓解鹰普邪教氛围的良药。 约翰斯通的论点是这样的：“如果我们能够抛开西方文化的束缚，让自己像孩子一样淫荡和自由，我们就会被带到一个更美好、更美丽的世界”。 好吧，你试过了，但团体中的女性仍然被降级为扮演荡妇和家庭主妇，仍然存在琐碎的个人斗争，而且大师在这里破坏工会8。 启蒙应该是什么样子？

我认为对约翰斯通最慷慨的辩护是，他的团体并不是异常的邪教，而且他的一位即兴表演老师同行的任何类似叙述都会引发同样的危险信号。 也许即兴表演课程和团体（特别是在 70 年代和 80 年代）总体上只是一种崇拜？ 现在已经读了四本关于约翰斯通的书，我不愿意再去读更多的书来证明或反驳这个理论，但它至少是合理的。

邪教和初创公司

对于任何熟悉旧金山软件工程文化的人来说，应该很清楚鹰普为何如此受欢迎。 初创公司和邪教之间的界限确实很细。

彼得·蒂尔（Peter Thiel）在他的著作《从零到一》中提出了一句名言：优秀的初创企业是“稍微不那么极端的邪教”。 如果您相信这一点，那么将 Impro 指定为 Palantir 新员工的必读读物是完全有道理的。 它告诉他们你想要经营什么样的邪教：你将无视现有的文化规范，学会玩好地位游戏，独立思考，并且通常会被大师塑造成一个更有说服力、更有效的工程师。

批判性地阅读，鹰普还可以作为工程师的手册，帮助他们试图识别自己所处的环境是否类似于邪教。 您的公司是否要求您重塑自己的个性，以便更好地完成工作？ 您是否被一位魅力非凡、地位崇高的领导者迷住了？ 你的公司是否试图让你处于一种毫无疑问的心流恍惚状态？

结论

在限制性文化的枷锁与上师光荣的自由之间的伟大战斗中，我永远站在限制性文化的枷锁一边。 总的来说，我认为大多数无聊和愚蠢的社会规范（例如不催眠和与你的学生结婚）都有重要的目的，不应该仅仅以自由的名义被废除。

Impro仍然是一本好书。 约翰斯通对权力动态、教育和创造力的分析有很多值得学习的地方。 众所周知，他非常擅长教学生如何即兴创作。 但我不建议将其作为你的人生哲学，并且我建议对任何过度推动这本书的人持怀疑态度。 摆脱现有的社会结构可能会让像约翰斯通这样自信、极具魅力的大师受益，但我们大多数人只是普通动物，在受规范约束的群体中表现得更好。

  1. 公平地说，约翰斯通引用了希拉·基辛格（Sheila Kitzinger）的《分娩经历》来支持这一说法（其他的只是他用自己的话来说的），所以也许他觉得这有点过分了。 正如您所料，分娩的疼痛是一个普遍的生物学事实。 ↩
  2. 令人担忧的是，《Something Like a Drug》（在前言中）中的描述表明该课程是非官方的。 ↩
  3. 举例来说，他让蒙面学生放松，然后用镜子惊吓他以触发恍惚状态。 ↩
  4. 可能是雅克·乐考克。 ↩
  5. 请参阅《基思·约翰斯通：批判传记》第 83 页。 ↩
  6. 我认为这是多余的。 ↩
  7. 在《基思·约翰斯通：批评传记》第 51 页（它被称为“批评”，但显然是在约翰斯通的参与和支持下撰写的，并且在任何时候都没有严重批评他）。 在《诀窍》中，汤姆发表了一段关于如何教孩子弹钢琴的独白，这可以直接从鹰普中借鉴。 ↩
  8. 1983 年，约翰斯通向那些计划成立工会的即兴演奏者“宣读防暴法案”，威胁说他们将被永远踢出乐队。 引用当时反对工会的团体成员丹尼斯·卡希尔的话：“我只是不明白这样做有什么意义。...... 我真的不认为有必要对抗基思或给基思带来问题或以任何方式让他不安，因为像谁有权力或谁没有权力这样简单的事情。” ↩

独裁者手册和技术能力的政治独裁者手册是一本雄心勃勃的书。 在引言中，其作者布鲁斯·布埃诺·德·梅斯基塔和阿拉斯泰尔·史密斯将自己定位为孙子和尼科洛·马基雅维利的继承者：为未来的成功领导者提供不带感情色彩的建议。鉴于此，我预计这本书会与《权力的48条法则》类似，但这并没有给我留下深刻的印象。 与许多自助书籍一样，《权力的 48 条法则》是“空卡路里”：读起来很有趣，但并不是真正有用或有启发性。 然而，《独裁者手册》是一本合法的政治学著作，是对政府实际学术理论的通俗介绍。继续阅读...
